name: Platform-neutral procurement language for AltID interoperability
consequences: Direct effect: Procurement documents will mandate platform-neutral authentication terms and AltID interoperability using open standards. Downstream implication: this creates a credible pathway for fallback paths and reduces vendor lock-in, but it can extend procurement timelines and provoke pushback from incumbents tied to current platform-dependent terms.
options: Codify a platform-neutral authentication requirement in procurement documents, mandating AltID interoperability with open standards and independent certification to reduce dependence on Apple and Google signals.

Institute a phased procurement framework that prioritizes platform-neutral fallback components as non-production pilots before any live MitID integration, balancing continuity needs with vendor-transition risks.
Establish a cross-border purchasing caucus to align terminology and clauses with EU expectations on platform neutrality, ensuring enforceability and governance oversight across agencies.
review: Core tension: procurement agility vs. platform neutrality; weakness: options assume cross-border coordination will be timely and legally straightforward.

name: Standards-driven fallback demonstrator program
consequences: Direct effect: resources focus on credible, standards-based demonstrations for platform-neutral access. Downstream implication: demonstrates feasibility and builds stakeholder confidence, but may not translate into immediate procurement wins and could expand scope beyond initial intent.
options: Develop a standards-first mobile demonstrator on a privacy-respecting platform using FIDO2/WebAuthn and OpenID Connect to prove a viable platform-neutral authentication pathway.
Pilot a browser-based fallback path with hardware tokens and a lightweight identity gateway to decouple trust anchors from mobile OS ecosystems.
Create an open, non-production reference implementation mapping API contracts and governance processes for AltID interoperability to quantify integration friction.
review: Core tension: demonstration scope vs organizational readiness; weakness: options may understate regulatory or certification bottlenecks that delay even non-production pilots.

name: EU-aligned interoperability push and regulatory engagement
consequences: Direct effect: formal engagement with EU and NSIS/eIDAS2 frameworks can create binding expectations for platform neutrality; downstream: alignment helps future procurement but may provoke resistance from incumbent platforms and require significant political capital.
options: Lead a Danish-EU standards dialogue to align AltID with NSIS, eIDAS2, and European Wallet architectures, securing commitments to platform-neutral requirements.
Publish a regulatory impact note detailing continuity obligations, supplier-concentration risks, and fallback expectations to inform procurement revisions.
Commission an external legal and policy review to identify obstacles and propose concrete amendments to support resilient authentication requirements.
review: Core tension: EU momentum may outpace national capability; weakness: options rely on regulatory progress that could stall or face legal challenges.

name: Coalition governance and oversight framework
consequences: Direct effect: establishes a governance layer to monitor progress and maintain accountability; downstream: increases transparency and legitimacy but could slow decision-making and invite politicization.
options: Formalize a cross-sector coalition including civil society, privacy advocates, academics, and policymakers to monitor procurement and advocate platform neutrality.
Establish a parliamentary digital policy working group with defined milestones to oversee AltID neutrality initiatives and escalate blockers.
Create a public accountability framework with transparent decision gates, independent technical reviews, and public reporting on progress and risk.
review: Core tension: governance speed vs accountability; weakness: options may overestimate political feasibility or understate resource constraints within agencies.

name: Technical risk management and resilience design
consequences: Direct effect: emphasizes modular design and separation of concerns to isolate platform dependencies; downstream: improves resilience but increases upfront architectural effort and may constrain rapid delivery.
options: Adopt a modular architectural approach that documents dependency surfaces and designs clean separations to enable rapid reconfiguration if platform policies shift.
Establish independent security testing and red-team exercises on fallback paths to identify abuse vectors and resilience gaps.
Implement data governance practices to minimize propagation of platform telemetry and reduce vendor-specific data leakage through the identity stack.
review: Core tension: resilience ambitions clash with funding and schedule; weakness: options may not address real-time policy shifts or supply chain disruptions.

name: Funding governance and program management
consequences: Direct effect: provides structured funding and oversight to sustain work across phases; downstream: reduces risk of funding gaps but may invite bureaucracy and slower decision gates, with a need to align with EU procurement rules.
options: Design a staged funding plan with milestone-based reviews, contingency reserves, and external audits to sustain momentum through financing uncertainty.
Create a dedicated procurement-readiness fund supporting neutral vendors and early-stage demonstration work independent of MitID incumbents.
Negotiate performance-based financing tied to EU-aligned deliverables and published standards to manage risk and incentivize concrete outcomes.
review: Core tension: funding discipline vs project flexibility; weakness: options may underestimate political or departmental resistance to earmarked funds.

name: Platform-agnostic identity assertion standards adoption
consequences: Direct effect: procurement and supplier landscape begin prioritizing platform-neutral identity assertions, expanding the pool of capable vendors. A downstream implication is that legacy, platform-dependent processes must be revised, creating short-term project-management frictions but longer-term resilience through optionality. The change may delay initial contract awards as standards are codified and tested, while reducing exposure to single-vendor dependence over time.
options: Adopt a formal policy mandating platform-agnostic identity assertions across all new AltID contracts, ensuring FIDO2/WebAuthn and OpenID Connect as baseline authentication methods and requiring vendor interoperability testing.
Launch a standards-working group to codify a platform-neutral trust model, baseline security requirements, and interoperability test suites for future identity wallets.
Pilot a cross-platform SDK with open references for device attestation and secure element usage to enable independent authentication flows.
review: Core tension: standardization pressures collide with incumbent platform-owner incentives, risking slow adoption and partial compliance. A weakness the options miss: the political economy of platform dependencies and the likelihood that procurement reform stalls before alignment; the options do not explicitly address institutional vetoes or timeline risk.

name: Formal procurement clauses to incentivize supplier diversification
consequences: Direct effect: procurement documents steer toward diversified supplier bases, reducing single-source risk. Downstream: bidders may form consortia or share capabilities, which can raise coordination costs and potentially lengthen procurement timelines. The diversification also increases the likelihood of sustained support across changes in government without being locked into a single vendor.
options: Embed requirements for multiple qualified suppliers and platform-neutral fallback paths in all AltID-related procurement documents, with clear exit and transition provisions.
Institute staged procurement gates tied to demonstrator milestones and independent security reviews to limit single-vendor risk.
Mandate open-source reference implementations and dynamic vendor-rotation strategies to dilute platform dependence across procurement cycles.
review: Core tension: competition induction vs continuity of service; weakness: the options do not fully account for lobbying by incumbents or ongoing maintenance costs of multiple suppliers.

name: Open standards-based demonstrators portfolio for fallback authentication
consequences: Direct effect: produces credible technical evidence for fallback viability; downstream: results may not translate directly into procurement-ready components, requiring additional gating and funding. The demonstrators also provide a tangible basis for EU-level discussions on interoperability and resilience.
options: Develop two non-production demonstrators, one mobile-based and one browser-hardware-token-based, to validate fallback paths against NSIS-compliant targets.
Document evidence and publish non-sensitive results to EU working groups to accelerate acceptance of platform-neutral fallbacks.
Coordinate independent security reviews of demonstrators to ensure credible evaluation and avoid overpromising capabilities.
review: Core tension: credibility of demonstrations vs real-world procurement constraints; weakness: options do not specify how to handle potential gaps between demonstration environments and operational MitID.

name: EU Digital Identity Wallet alignment and influence strategy
consequences: Direct effect: improves legitimacy and pressure from EU alignment; downstream: national policy may resist changes perceived as external influence, risking slow uptake. The leverage here is to shape EU-level standards in a way that supports platform-neutrality while safeguarding Denmark's policy space.
options: Engage EU bodies to propose platform-neutral language in Digital Identity Wallet specifications and issue briefs advocating for fallback routes.
Publish a policy note on convergence points between NSIS, eIDAS, and EU wallet requirements to reduce national-level divergence.
Organize a cross-border stakeholder roundtable to align procurement language with EU standards and accelerate adoption.
review: Core tension: top-down EU alignment vs domestic procurement sovereignty; weakness: the options do not address possible misalignment in national implementation timelines.

name: Resilience-focused procurement governance with milestone gating
consequences: Direct effect: governance discipline reduces risk of late-stage failures; downstream: adds administrative burden and may slow rapid decisions under political pressure. Milestone gates create clarity but require robust oversight capacity to avoid bottlenecks.
options: Institute milestone-based funding releases and independent verification checkpoints to ensure progression toward platform neutrality while preserving accountability.
Create a transparent risk register and escalation path for procurement blockers to maintain momentum despite political or budgetary shifts.
Embed continuity planning into every phase, including exit strategies and data portability requirements for potential fallback architectures.
review: Core tension: governance rigidity vs agile policy evolution; weakness: the options may not address urgent blockers and require long lead times to realize.

name: Independent certification pathway for platform-neutral trust anchors
consequences: Direct effect: provides credible validation for platform-neutral anchors; downstream: certification costs and timelines may constrain procurement and delay rollout. The pathway signals credibility for alternative trust anchors and reduces perceived risk for public stakeholders.
options: Commission an independent certification process for alternative trust anchors, including attestations of device attestation and cross-platform interoperability.
Define a lightweight accreditation scheme for fallback authenticators to gain quick market access while maintaining security baselines.
Pilot a regulatory-compliant certification framework to compare platform-dependent vs platform-neutral anchors with objective criteria.
review: Core tension: certification overhead vs practical deployment; weakness: the options do not specify funding or governance for the certification process.

name: Fallback authentication pathway integration in AltID procurement language
consequences: Direct effect: creates a formal requirement for platform-neutral fallback authentication to be considered in AltID procurements. It drives cross-vendor interoperability and reduces single-vendor risk, but may slow procurement cycles due to new validation steps and stakeholder negotiation. A downstream implication is higher governance overhead and potential schedule delays as standards are implemented.
options: Embed a formal fallback authentication requirement into AltID procurement language, specify minimal interoperability standards, and reserve budget for independent testing and certification.
Create staged procurement approach obliging platform-neutral fallback mechanisms to be evaluated in pilot contracts before full-scale rollout.
Partition procurement blocks to treat platform-neutral fallback as a primary criterion, with independent certification and ongoing audit requirements.
review: Core tension centers on embedding fallback requirements into procurement potentially delaying procurement cycles and provoking resistance from incumbents. A weakness the options miss is how to ensure privacy and data minimization are preserved in any new fallback pathway, and how to align with cross-border eIDAS NSIS obligations.

name: Independent verifier framework for platform-neutral trust anchors
consequences: Direct effect: creates independent verification of platform-neutral trust anchors against NSIS/eIDAS benchmarks, boosting credibility and reducing vendor lock-in. Downstream, it can raise upfront costs and extend timelines for procurement and certification, and may require governance to manage conflicts of interest. This approach improves transparency but requires robust governance to prevent capture.
options: Establish an independent certification body to validate platform-neutral trust anchors against NSIS and eIDAS requirements, with transparent scoring and public reports.
Commission neutral interoperability tests across multiple device ecosystems, including privacy-respecting stacks, to demonstrate resilience under varied threat models.
Publish a living conformance suite with ongoing surveillance, enabling iterative improvements while preventing vendor drift.
review: Core tension: certification may lag behind rapid platform updates; weakness: the options don't address governance of the verifier itself or conflicts of interest.

name: Procurement incentives to broaden supplier base and reduce concentration
consequences: Direct effect: injects competition by favoring multiple qualified suppliers for fallback components to avoid single-provider dependency and improve resilience. Downstream, it could increase short-term cost and risk of inconsistent capabilities if standards are not harmonized, and may necessitate more widespread supplier onboarding.
options: Introduce procurement criteria that actively favor multiple qualified suppliers for fallback components to avoid single-provider dependency and improve resilience.
Offer multi-vendor pilot contracts with shared risk and clear exit clauses to prevent lock-in and provide transition options.
Institute a transparent performance and capability tracking regime that compares alternative signals without compromising privacy protections.
review: Core tension: incumbents may resist open competition; weakness: the options may not ensure continuity during transitions or avoid gaps if standards diverge.

name: Regulatory engagement with NSIS/eIDAS for resilience mandates
consequences: Direct effect: aligns Danish resilience goals with EU frameworks and potentially unlocks funding or regulatory support. Downstream, policymakers may impose additional reporting requirements and implementation timelines, potentially increasing compliance burden.
options: Lead a formal regulatory engagement with NSIS and eIDAS to codify resilience and platform-neutral access in future policy instruments.
Coordinate EU-wide pilots and working groups to harmonize fallback pathways with cross-border identity wallets and standards.
Draft policy proposals clarifying accountability for continuity of service in identity ecosystems during platform outages and vendor failures.
review: Core tension: policy alignment often lags technical feasibility; weakness: options may assume favorable regulatory reception without addressing domestic political obstacles.

name: Coalition-driven transparency and accountability program
consequences: Direct effect: builds public trust and legitimacy; downstream, it imposes reporting burdens and may expose sensitive political negotiations, potentially constraining negotiation flexibility.
options: Establish a public coalition portal publishing risk assessments, stakeholder positions, and decision gates for AltID-related procurement.
Require regular independent audits of supplier concentration risk, continuity plans, and procurement outcomes with public summaries.
Create a governance charter with clear escalation paths, timelines, and review events for decisions affecting fallback paths.
review: Core tension: transparency can reveal sensitive procurement positions; weakness: options do not specify data-handling protections or redaction rules.

name: Non-production demonstrator portfolio to de-risk platform-neutral claims
consequences: Direct effect: demonstrates feasibility of platform-neutral approaches without production risk. Downstream, it consumes project resources and may be dismissed as non-representative without a formal transition plan.
options: Develop two non-production demonstrators—one mobile-oriented and one browser-based—focusing on standard protocols and user flows without live MitID integration.
Document architecture decisions and threat models in a living repository to enable external validation and community review.
Open-source core components under permissive licenses to accelerate independent verification and stakeholder engagement.
review: Core tension: demonstrators risk being dismissed as cosmetic if not tied to credible transition steps; weakness: the options do not specify how feedback from demonstrations will influence procurement language.
